speaker: interviewer
text: Were in Sweden, you were-- what part of Sweden were you in initially? What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [INAUDIBLE]. It's very hard. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we arrived from Denmark, that was [INAUDIBLE]. But from there, they took us right away to different villages to put us in different places, because there were 20 or 25 people together. And there were 1,000, 2,000 who arrived. So the next place we did go was [INAUDIBLE]. And I was there after recovering of my illness, going to doctor, from that place till January 10, till [INAUDIBLE], where I started to teach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that that you started to teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you start to teach?

words.text: year did you start to teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: 194, the end of January. So I was about six months in Sweden. You, you count better from me. Till January is seven months, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Roughly.

speaker: interviewee
text: May 3-- May-- seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And after seven months, I did started to work as a teacher. I was teaching there two and a half years. And some of my pupils lives here in United State, I told you about. And we are still keeping our contact, talking. And one of them is living in Santiago, the Chile, South America, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years did you say in Sweden for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did stay till 1951, April, when I did come to Canada. I lived in Canada four years, waiting for the permission to come to United States, because I waited ten years till they allowed me with the Hungarian quota. And that really hurt me, because all the SS men were here in United States in 1945 and 1946. Matter of fact, Dr. Joseph Mengele also came to New York and has family in today United States. And that hurt me very much, because I suddenly became for United States a Hungarian. But still, the best country on Earth is United States. And I hope the people will keep it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, April, I met my husband in Sweden in a convalescent home. Because I was waiting for my brother, who was in Hungary, and trying him to come out and did go to the Swedish king to have the permission. And that was way before. And I did to go to the king for the permission. And we did received it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he got it in Hungary, they told him that he'll never leave the country alive. And they killed him. I was in, in Mal-- Landskrona-- that is a city in, in Sweden-- waiting for my brother. And when I found out he was taken off from the airplane, I collapsed. And I hit my head. And I got a brain-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you know your brain shake? Tom, where are you? You know, when someone hits the head and the brain is-- anyway-- Concussion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Concussion. Thank you. And I was very ill. So I was sent to a convalescent home. And that's the place where my husband came also. He came from Germany, and other people who had no one, nowhere to go. That convalescent home was kept by the Jewish organization. And there, people could, put, you know temporarily, and there where we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did get married in 1950, December 24, in Stockholm. And we are married ever since, thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is your husband from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, where is your husband from? Where was he originally from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's from Hungary. And the name of the city is Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll meet him after, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have three children. God bless them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two girls and one boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is Susan, Nancy, and Thomas. And my oldest one, Susan, is married and has two children and a wonderful husband, thank God. And the other two is single.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm. I have a hard time to say. You want to know about them as people, as person, or what they are doing? I will tell you. My oldest one in, in Washington, DC, she's a psychiatric social worker and therapist and several other degree. And she's a wonderful person. And Nancy and Tom is living here in Los Angeles. I think they are wonderful children, wonderful human beings. I am sorry, but they are affected by the fact that the parents are survivors. Because unknowingly, we affected them with our fearness or, or cautiousness or mistrust or things like that. We didn't ever told them when they were little that we were in concentration camp. They were asking where are they grandparents or my sister or my brothers. And I have to tell them that they are dead. They died in the war. But they were about 10 years old or even older when I explained that they were in concentration camp. Because I didn't wanted our children to be burdened with that and feeling different from the rest of their schoolmates. That was the reason we didn't tell. But still, unknowingly, they did felt something different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elisabeth, is there anything that you would like to say now to your children or to your grandchildren or your great-grandchildren to be, future generations, or just anybody that may be watching this tape about your experiences? Any messages that you want to deliver?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My messages is-- and the reason I am going to the Wiesenthal Center and colleges and churches to speak-- to fight for freedom and peace, not with your fists, but with your vote. You should know whom you are appointing to any office, who is your president, who is your mayor, who is your police chief, and whoever in a leadership. And you should fight against prejudice. I think that is the most important things on Earth-- to stop hatred, not to allow people to have groups like the Skinheads, like KKK, or gangs, anything connecting hatred or selection out people from each other and, and stamping people, you are black or you are a Jew or you are Catholic or Protestant or whatever reason. That should stop. That's what I would like to let the world know and help me and help each other to have that goal reached.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This picture are from my grandparents-- My grandfather, whose name was Josef Kain. And he was a bodyguard for Queen Elizabeth. And my aunts and uncles-- the third one is my mother, Charlotte,

speaker: interviewer
text: Third from which end, from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: From, from the left. And I don't know all the names, but I know that is [? Eleanor. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- the first one in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is the first in the front. And the second girl is Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's sitting next to your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is sitting in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is my Uncle [INAUDIBLE]. And that is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Wearing-- top right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the top on the right corner is [INAUDIBLE]. And the next one is [? Mike. ?] And the third one is [? Shandor. ?] And the fourth one is [INAUDIBLE]. And then is my mother and [? Margie ?] and [? Bertha. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think approximately 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Marg-- that was taken in so-called [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the name that was Hungary then, and later became Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. That is the picture of my parents, my mother, whose name was Charlotte Kain Mohr, and my father, Joseph Mohr. That picture was taken in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the year 1910-- 1911 or '12, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city do you think it was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kecskemet. That is my sister, Claire. She was born in 1915. And she died in Bergen-Belsen, shot by an SS man three days before the English soldiers liberated Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the photo was taken in Kecskemet, in my city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. And what year do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about 1942 or '40-- 1942. This picture is-- I have to make a correction. That picture was made 1939 in Kecskemet, in my city. Here is my older brother. His name is Imra. And the picture was sent to my younger brother by him, 1941, in-- from Hungary, before he was taken to the Russian front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what in this picture is so important that we need take notice of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is very important, because the yellow armband is on him and the uniform. Later, they took off the uniforms from the Jewish soldiers. And they had to supply their own clothes. And that was a horrible burden on the parents. Because that was almost impossible to get any new stuff during the war and was humiliated tremendously because the uniform was taken away. That is a picture from my brother after the war in Kecskemet, 1945, December.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older-- I didn't said older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name? Tell us his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Imra. You want me to-- older brother, his name is Imra. We saw a picture from him in the soldier's uniform. And that picture was done after the war. He's the one who was killed by a Hungarian communist because not joining the communist group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a piece of soap. I received that in Denmark, 1945, May 3, from the doctor in the hospital and the nurses gave us when I left the train coming from Germany from concentration camp, going towards Sweden to freedom. And I missed the train. The doctor took-- and the nurses take care of me, cleaned me, and gave me that piece of soap. And I never could use it. I wanted to have it as a memento. This is a picture of my three children and my son-in-law. Left to right is my son Tom, Thomas Mann. Next to him is my oldest daughter, Susan Mann, now Biehl. And next to it, is her husband, Richard Biehl. Next to him is my other daughter, Nancy Mann. Taken the photo in Washington, DC, at my Susan's wedding day in the Hay-Adams Hotel, where the wedding was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1986. This picture is my little granddaughter. Her name is Grace Biehl. And she will be six years old this year, 1995, December 19. She lives in Washington, DC, with my daughter and my son-in-law and with her brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo was taken last year, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in Washington, DC, in May. This picture is taken from my grandson. His name is Nick Biehl. Was taken in Washington, DC, in their home outside in the patio. He is three years old. He's a wonderful little guy. I love him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say what year this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, 1995, in, in September-- that was his birthday. This is a picture of my husband and myself. It's a picture of us taken in 1991. Unfortunately, he already was sick. Soon, we will go into the room. And we'll make picture with him together. He's bedridden. He has cancer in the spine and in the kidney. And unfortunately, he's paralyzed from the waist down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the name of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name is-- husband's name is Rudy Mann. And my name is Elisabeth Mann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where, where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in Los Angeles in a party. It was a celebration for somebody's son's bar mitzvah. This picture was done after the war, right after the war, about June. My hobby was, before the war, to painting. And after the war when the Hungarian Jewish group came to ask questions in the convalescent home and asked what we really would like to have, I asked for pencil and drawing paper. And I did received it. And that was the time when I made that picture, remembering one of the men in the concentration camp. This is a picture of a lady who was in the ghetto together with me. And she was terrible upset. By the end, she suicidal. And I never could get out of her-- out of my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- No, I don't remember, know her name. But I did know then when I did make the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you make this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947, I am sorry, in, in Sweden. Then, I was already teaching in the school. And I did still do some drawing-- or did some drawing, whichever is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elisabeth, can you please introduce these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One is missing of my children. That is Susan, who is in Washington, DC, with her family, Rick, her husband, and Gracie and Nick, her children. And here, I am sitting with my daughter, Nancy, and my son, Tom. And they are wonderful people. I am very, very proud of them. And I feel very, very blessed to having them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would either of you like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say a few words. It's hard to summarize the feelings I have for my parents in just a few words, but I'll try. Certainly, they've had some very difficult challenges in life. And I think that the strength that I have has come from them, their I'm sorry-- [CRYING] their will to survive, their determination to never give up. I have found that to be very inspirational. And the respect and love that I have for them is more than I can describe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's pretty hard to follow. [LAUGHING] And I feel pretty much the same way as my sister does. And while it hasn't been particularly easy having parents who were survivors, and I know when I was a child, I was embarrassed by it, as an adult, I'm proud and am inspired by their strength and their ability to not only survive, but to create a life for themselves in a new country with a new language. And I know that whenever things are difficult for me, I think about what my parents have gone through. And it pretty much minimizes virtually anything that I have experienced and makes my life not quite as difficult. And I'm incredibly proud of the work that my mother does with Wiesenthal Center and, and educating thousands of people as to what happened. And I think her work is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the new tape?
